6 min read
Just now

--

Three views of our AI moment, and the gentler questions they raise: the promise, the reality, and who it serves.
It is easy, and not wrong, to be moved by the big hopeful vision for artificial intelligence. Stories of someone finding an answer that had been slow to arrive, or of expert guidance reaching people who never had it before, point toward a kinder and more equitable world. That promise is real, and I do not want to wave it away.
But the promise is only the first of three views I have been holding together lately. The second is how people actually use AI once it is in their hands. The third, quieter one, is about who sits behind the tools we are coming to rely on. Three recent pieces, read side by side, fill in all three, and together they ask more human questions than the usual noise around AI.
The promise: expertise within reach
In his essay “What if everyone could afford an expert?”, Reid Hoffman describes a future where having a personal AI health guide, legal guide, and tutor becomes as ordinary as carrying a phone. Just as the smartphone put knowledge in everyone’s pocket, he argues, AI can put a kind of operationalised expertise there too: knowledge brought to bear on your particular situation, in the moment you need it.
His case rests on stories like Lauren Bannon’s. After months of uncertainty about what was wrong, she described her symptoms to a chatbot, which suggested a specific test. That test led to a diagnosis of thyroid cancer, and she has since credited the tool with helping her catch it. For anyone who has waited anxiously for answers about their own health, or watched a loved one wait, the appeal here is not abstract. It touches a very human wish to be heard and helped a little sooner.
The reality: how people are really turning to AI
The most grounded picture of how AI is actually used comes from Harvard Business Review’s “AI in the Wild,” the annual study by Marc Zao-Sanders and Sara Biuk. This year they looked at 12,637 real uses gathered from public conversation across the web. Two things stand out, and both are gentler than the headlines suggest.
The first is what people most often want from AI. For the second year running, the single most common use is not coding or analysis but companionship and emotional support, now about 11% of all use, up from 5% the year before. People give their assistants names, talk to them about their relationships, and reach for them in lonely or worried moments. It would be easy to read this as a problem. I read it, mostly, as a quiet signal of how much support people are looking for, and how hard that can sometimes be to find. It is also tender ground, and the researchers are careful to pass on a clinician’s reminder that a general assistant, however comforting, is not a substitute for trained mental health care, especially when that support is hard to reach.
The second is a soft caution the authors name “thinkslop”: how easily, when a capable tool is right there, we let it begin our thinking for us. We send a prompt before we have worked out what we actually want to say. We reach for AI first and skip the slightly uncomfortable, generative work of sitting with a problem. We accept polished output and, because drafting is itself a form of thinking, quietly miss the thinking. And because these tools are designed to keep us engaged, their steady encouragement can leave us feeling more certain than we have earned. None of this makes anyone foolish. It is simply very human, and the same study is warm about the other side: used as a sparring partner, asked to challenge us and poke holes in our reasoning, AI can make our thinking sharper rather than softer. As one person in the study put it, it works best as a mirror, not a genie.
The reality at work: a gentler kind of tiredness
There is a version of this at work, too, and it deserves compassion rather than alarm. A study from Boston Consulting Group describes something many of us will recognise and calls it “AI brain fry”: the particular tiredness that comes from supervising AI all day, checking and rechecking its output, holding several tools in your head at once. It affects around 14% of users, who tend to make more significant mistakes and to feel a stronger pull to step away. The strain comes less from the work itself than from the quiet effort of overseeing a confident machine.
The third view: who are these tools really serving?
The last view widens the lens kindly. On Mishal Husain’s show, Signal’s president Meredith Whittaker offers a thoughtful reflection on the next phase of all this: the AI agents now being offered as personal assistants that book, message, and manage on our behalf. To do that well, an agent needs a great deal of access to our accounts, our messages, our lives. Whittaker’s invitation is not to fear the technology but to stay mindful of the trust we are extending, and to whom, and to remember that a little privacy is part of what lets us think, question, and be ourselves. It is a caring reframe of the whole conversation: not only whether we use AI wisely, but whether the tools we lean on are built to look after us in return.
Holding the three together
Placed beside one another, these views feel less like an argument and more like a fuller, more honest picture.
Hoffman is right about the promise, and the life-changing moment is real and will become more common. The research is right too, that access on its own is not yet integration, and not yet the same as benefit. AI tends to amplify whatever we bring to it, our clarity and our blind spots alike. Met as a mirror, it can sharpen us and give us back time. Leaned on as a substitute, it can gently thin the very capacities, and the confidence, we hoped it would strengthen.
What this might mean for us
This is where the real opportunity sits, and it is a hopeful one. The question was never whether people can reach AI; increasingly, they can. The question is whether we let it sit inside our judgment rather than in place of it. In practice that can be gentle and ordinary: giving our own thinking a proper run before we open a prompt; noticing which parts of our work and our inner life are better kept human, and bringing the tender, high-stakes things to people who are trained to hold them; shaping how we work so the tools lighten the load rather than add to it; and staying quietly curious about who makes the tools and what they do with what we share.
In an era where answers are abundant, the real edge is softer and more human than it sounds. It is knowing which questions are worth asking, holding the judgment to act on them with care, and staying gently awake to the system doing the answering. That is harder than adopting a chatbot. It is also the version of all this that actually leaves us more capable, and more ourselves.
Sources: Reid Hoffman, “What if everyone could afford an expert?”; Marc Zao-Sanders, “How People Are Really Using AI in 2026” (Harvard Business Review); Bedard et al., “When Using AI Leads to ‘Brain Fry’” (Harvard Business Review); and Meredith Whittaker on The Mishal Husain Show.
